metadata.summary: Patterns for Rails ActiveRecord optimization: Query Objects for complex filter chains, named scopes for simple primitives, eager loading with includes/preload/eager_load, and N+1 prevention.

content.title: ActiveRecord Patterns for Rails
content.summary: Patterns for Rails ActiveRecord optimization: Query Objects for complex filter chains, named scopes for simple primitives, eager loading with includes/preload/eager_load, and N+1 prevention. Covers model callbacks, find_each for bulk operations, and mandatory eager-load-by-default in controllers. Use the bullet + prosopite gems to gate N+1 regressions in CI.

why.p: Lazy loading is ActiveRecord's primary production trap. A missing includes in a controller that renders a list fires N additional SELECT statements — invisible in code review and undetected without a dedicated N+1 detector. Query Objects extract complex filter logic from controllers into testable, composable units under app/queries/.

list.item: Rails apps where models exceed ~150 lines and inline where chains scatter across controllers.
Multi-tenant Rails apps where global scopes and tenant-aware queries must be enforced consistently.
Measured ORM hot paths (>30% of request time in AR) — query objects + eager loading measurably improve.
LLM-driven feature work where each AR query must be reviewable in isolation.
Codebases adopting Trailblazer / dry-rb partials — Query Objects play nicely as ingest into the new architecture.
Simple CRUD admin (Rails Admin / ActiveAdmin) — the framework manages queries; query objects add ceremony.
Rails apps under 1k LOC — direct AR scope blocks plus thin controllers are fine.
Read-replicas or sharded DBs needing raw connected_to(role: :reading) — query object DSL hides the switch.
Background workers operating on enqueued IDs via find_each — no benefit from a chained query object.
Reporting/analytics — drop to ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute with explicit SQL.

rule.statement: Query Objects must accept a relation in the constructor (default Model.all), chain methods that return self, and expose a terminal results method returning the relation — never an array.
rule.rationale: Returning self allows callers to compose filters. Returning the relation instead of to_a defers SQL execution and allows further scoping at the controller level.

rule.statement: Cap Query Objects at approximately 10 chainable methods; if more are needed, split by use case into multiple query objects.
rule.rationale: A 30-method query object that covers every one-off filter becomes a god class — harder to read than inline where chains and impossible to test in isolation.

example.title: Fluent Query Object with chainable methods
example.code:
# app/queries/users_query.rb class UsersQuery def initialize(relation = User.all) @relation = relation end
def active @relation = @relation.where(active: true) self end
def with_role(role) @relation = @relation.where(role: role) self end
def search(term) return self if term.blank? @relation = @relation.where( 'name ILIKE :term OR email ILIKE :term', term: "%#{term}%" ) self end
def ordered @relation = @relation.order(created_at: :desc) self end
def with_associations @relation = @relation.includes(:profile, :roles) self end
def paginate(page:, per_page: 20) @relation = @relation.page(page).per(per_page) self end
def results @relation end end
# Controller usage users = UsersQuery.new .active .with_role('admin') .search(params[:q]) .with_associations .ordered .paginate(page: params[:page]) .results

antipattern.description: includes vs eager_load confusion: User.includes(:posts).where('posts.flagged = true') without references(:posts).
antipattern.reason: includes heuristically picks strategy; adding a where on the joined table silently switches to eager_load (one big LEFT OUTER JOIN). Use references(:posts) or split the query explicitly to make the join type visible.

rule.statement: Eager-load at the controller boundary, never in views. A view triggering a query is invisible in code review.
rule.rationale: View-triggered queries are rendered as template output, not as SQL log entries in the request cycle that code reviewers check. Eager loading in controllers makes query cost reviewable.

rule.statement: Avoid default_scope; use named scopes (scope :active) and force callers to opt in.
rule.rationale: A default_scope { where(deleted_at: nil) } is invisible at every call site. Agents debugging "missing data" rarely think to call withoutGlobalScopes — it costs an hour per incident.

rule.statement: Use find_each / in_batches for any loop over 1k+ records; never User.all.each.
rule.rationale: User.all.each loads the entire result set into memory. find_each streams in batches of 1000, keeping memory constant.

example.title: Model with named scopes and proper callbacks
example.code:
class User < ApplicationRecord has_one :profile, dependent: :destroy has_many :orders, dependent: :nullify has_many :roles, through: :user_roles
validates :email, presence: true, uniqueness: { case_sensitive: false } validates :name, presence: true, length: { minimum: 2, maximum: 100 }
# Named scopes — callers opt in scope :active, -> { where(active: true) } scope :admins, -> { joins(:roles).where(roles: { name: 'admin' }) } scope :recent, -> { where('created_at > ?', 30.days.ago) }
before_validation :normalize_email after_create_commit :send_welcome_email
private
def normalize_email self.email = email&.downcase&.strip end
def send_welcome_email UserMailer.welcome(self).deliver_later end end

antipattern.description: update_all used on audited or paper-trailed models.
antipattern.reason: update_all bypasses callbacks silently — counter_cache, paper_trail, and audit logs break without any error. Use update! per record or explicitly disable callbacks with a comment explaining why.

section.name: mandatory-rules
section.p: Concrete rules for ActiveRecord safety that prevent common regressions:

list.item: Indexes for every where and order_by column; verify with lol_dba:missing_indexes after every migration.
counter_cache: true + touch: true on belongs_to; otherwise parent caches go stale.
Keep callbacks idempotent — after_create_commit may fire twice in edge cases.
pluck(:id) instead of map(&:id) — 100x faster at scale; lint via Rubocop Rails/Pluck.
Test scopes in isolation: RSpec.describe '.active' block; never only via the controller.
Force Postgres in CI from day 1; test DB != prod DB (JSON, full-text, case sensitivity).
scope only for parameterless or primitive queries. Anything taking a model/relation goes into a Query Object.
Counter caches with counter_cache: true + touch: true. Otherwise list endpoints recount every render.
includes for read paths; joins for filter-only paths (where you don't need the joined object). Document the choice in a comment.
Avoid update_all on audited or paper-trailed models. Use update! per record or temporarily disable callbacks explicitly.

antipattern.description: Hidden default_scope causes missing rows; agent writes User.where(...) and gets fewer results than expected.
antipattern.reason: Force agents to grep for default_scope before any query change. Agents debugging "missing data" rarely reach for withoutGlobalScopes.

antipattern.description: save vs save! — agent uses save and does not check return; record silently fails validation.
antipattern.reason: Standardize on save! + rescue at the boundary, or always check .persisted? after save.

antipattern.description: Memoization on a relation: @users ||= User.where(...) returns a relation that re-queries on each .each.
antipattern.reason: Memoize .to_a not the relation; otherwise memoization has no effect.

antipattern.description: Strong migrations bypass: add_index :users, :email blocks writes for minutes on large tables.
antipattern.reason: Use algorithm: :concurrently and disable_ddl_transaction! — strong_migrations gem enforces this in development.

antipattern.description: first without order is non-deterministic in Postgres under concurrent inserts.
antipattern.reason: Always specify an explicit order: User.order(:created_at).first — or use find(id) when you have the ID.

antipattern.description: N+1 by default. Lazy associations are AR's killer trap. Without includes / preload / eager_load, agents copy patterns straight into prod regressions.
antipattern.reason: Use Bullet and Prosopite gems to gate N+1 detections in CI. Run every test with both enabled.

antipattern.description: Callback chains explode. before_save → after_commit → after_create_commit-driven logic re-fires on tests, on fixture loads, and at the wrong transactional boundary.
antipattern.reason: Keep callbacks simple and idempotent. Test callbacks separately in isolation. Use after_create_commit not after_create for email/notification side effects.

antipattern.description: Caching invalidation gaps. AR cache_key_with_version invalidates on updated_at; agents forget touch: true on belongs_to, parent caches go stale.
antipattern.reason: Always add touch: true on belongs_to associations that have counter_cache. Test cache behavior explicitly.

antipattern.description: Query object growing into a god class. Agent adds a method per use case; the query object grows to 30 methods covering one-off filters.
antipattern.reason: Cap at ~10 chainable methods; if more needed, split by use case into multiple query objects. Review query objects for single responsibility.

antipattern.description: Polymorphic belongs_to typos. Wrong as: argument silently writes wrong type strings; integrity is not enforced.
antipattern.reason: Add a before_validation to enforce the type from a constant list. Test polymorphic associations in specs.

antipattern.description: Test DB ≠ prod DB. Agents test on sqlite, ship to Postgres; case sensitivity, JSON column behavior, and array types diverge.
antipattern.reason: Force Postgres in CI from day 1. Run migrations in CI and on a real Postgres instance before merging.

antipattern.description: Schema drift across branches. Two agents add migrations with the same timestamp on parallel branches; merge produces non-deterministic order.
antipattern.reason: Use bin/rails db:migrate:status in CI to catch timestamp collisions. Enforce migration naming to prevent conflicts.

antipattern.description: destroy cascade through dependent: :destroy can fan out to thousands of records.
antipattern.reason: Force agents to find_each + explicit destroy when chains are deep, or switch to soft delete + background cleanup job.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools for working with ActiveRecord:

list.item: rails generate model / rails db:migrate: Scaffold + migrate (bundled with Rails).
bin/rails console: Headless REPL for relation testing (bundled).
bullet: N+1 + unused eager-load detector (gem 'bullet').
prosopite: Newer N+1 detector, lower noise than Bullet (gem 'prosopite').
rails-erd: Generate ERD diagrams from schema (gem 'rails-erd').
strong_migrations: Block dangerous migrations (gem 'strong_migrations').
rubocop-rails / rubocop-rspec: Style + lint, runs in pre-commit (gem 'rubocop-rails').
bundle exec rake db:rollback: Reversibility check before commit (bundled).
lol_dba: Find missing indexes (gem 'lol_dba').
pg_query / EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Query plan inspection (Postgres builtins).

section.p: SaaS and OSS services that work with ActiveRecord:

list.item: Heroku Postgres / RDS / Neon: Managed Postgres with API support (agent-friendly).
Skylight / Scout APM / NewRelic: SaaS APM with per-AR-query timing (agent-friendly).
Sentry: SaaS error tracking; catches ActiveRecord::* exceptions (agent-friendly).
pgHero: OSS Postgres dashboard; slow queries, unused indexes (agent-friendly).
ActiveRecord-Reset: Helper for test isolation around default_scope (OSS).
Trailblazer: Operation/Query objects upgrade path (OSS).
dry-rb (dry-validation, dry-monads): Query object inputs validated; results as monads (OSS).

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive AR work as a multi-stage pipeline:

list.item: (1) A planner subagent reads app/models/*.rb and db/schema.rb and emits target query/scope changes.
(2) A code-writer subagent generates model + scopes + query object + RSpec specs in one pass.
(3) A perf subagent runs the test suite under bullet and prosopite and fails on any detection.
(4) A migration subagent generates indexes for new where-columns and verifies via EXPLAIN.

section.p: Persist changes as app/models/*.rb + app/queries/*.rb + db/migrate/*.rb; agents must touch all three or schema drifts.
section.code: You are a Rails 7.1 backend engineer. Move all `User.where(...)` chains in app/controllers/admin/users_controller.rb into a UsersQuery class under app/queries/. Output: query class, model scopes for primitives (active, search), updated controller. Keep each method ≤8 lines. Run: bundle exec rspec spec/queries/.

Run `BULLET=true bundle exec rspec`. For each Bullet warning, report: spec file, model, association, and propose `.includes(...)` on the relevant query. Do not modify specs; only modify controllers, queries, or models. Re-run until zero warnings.

template.purpose: CI gate: fail on N+1 (Bullet/Prosopite) and per-spec query budget.
